A CONSOLIDATED RESTRICTIONS IMMIGRATION ACT (1907–1965) — ROUGH DRAFT TEXT (U.S.-ONLY)

Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, Title

An Act to limit immigration admissions to the United States through inspection, medical and character exclusion, restrictive documentary requirements, and numerical limitation of admissions; to provide for exclusion, removal, and enforcement; and to consolidate immigration statutes enacted from and including the Act of 1907 through the Act of 1965 into a single comprehensive framework. Section 1. Definitions

For purposes of this Act:

The term “alien” means any person who is not a citizen or national of the United States.
The term “admission” means entry into the United States as an admitted alien under this Act.
The terms “excluded” and “inadmissible” mean barred from admission, parole, or entry under this Act.
The term “Secretary” means the Secretary charged with administration of immigration laws.
The term “carrier” means any vessel, aircraft, or other conveyance operator required by law to transport persons into the United States.
The term “quota” means an annual numerical limitation administered under this Act.
The term “admission category” means a broad pathway for admission authorized by this Act.
The term “admission class” means a specific implementable class within an admission category, with prescribed eligibility, documentary requirements, and quota-allocation rules.

Section 2. Inspection; Admission or Exclusion; Burden of Proof

Every arriving alien shall be inspected by immigration officers.
An alien shall not be admitted unless the officer determines both: (a) that the alien is eligible under the applicable admission category and admission class, including satisfaction of any quota and evidentiary requirements; and
(b) that the alien is not inadmissible under the medical exclusion, character exclusion, and documentary and proof requirements of this Act.
In all determinations under this Act, the alien shall bear the burden to prove eligibility and the absence of inadmissibility grounds.
An alien shall be excluded if evidence is insufficient, inconsistent, materially incomplete, unverifiable, or not in the form required by this Act or implementing regulations.

Section 3. Medical Exclusion (1907–1917 Style; Enumerated Disqualifying Medical Conditions)

An alien shall be excluded as inadmissible if, through medical examination and admissible evidence, the alien is found to have any listed disqualifying condition designated by this Act or regulations issued pursuant to this Act, including:

Communicable diseases specified as disqualifying by this Act or regulations, including contagious illnesses presenting a public health risk.
Any physical defect, disability, or abnormality specified as disqualifying, particularly where the condition is found to require substantial institutional care as defined by regulations.
Chronic conditions specified as disqualifying, where the examiner finds the condition likely to impair health or likely to require ongoing care beyond limits specified by regulations.
Mental disorders specified as disqualifying, including disorders deemed likely to pose harm to self or others, or likely to require institutional care as defined by regulations.
Substance-related conditions specified as disqualifying, including addiction or conditions judged likely to create public safety or public health risks under standards set by regulations.
Failure to undergo a required medical examination, or failure to provide required medical results in the form and time required by this Act or regulations.
Any other condition not specifically enumerated, for which the examiner finds under standards set by this Act or regulations that a public health or public safety risk exists that falls within the medical-disqualification criteria of this Act.

Section 4. Character Exclusion (1907–1917 Style; Enumerated Disqualifying Character)

An alien shall be excluded as inadmissible if, based on admissible evidence, the alien is found to be of disqualifying character under this Act, including:

Criminal conduct involving offenses enumerated by this Act or regulations, including violent offenses and offenses involving moral turpitude as defined by this Act or regulations.
Immoral or corrupt conduct as defined by this Act, including conduct deemed inconsistent with public order and welfare.
Prohibited associations, including membership in, active support for, or affiliation with organizations or persons designated as disqualifying by this Act or regulations.
Fraud and misrepresentation, including: (a) false statements regarding identity, relationship, qualifications, health, or prior immigration history; (b) concealment of material facts; (c) use of forged, counterfeit, or materially altered documents.
Other disqualifying conduct specified by this Act or regulations, including attempts or conspiracies to violate immigration requirements where such conduct is treated as character-disqualifying under this Act.

Section 5. Documentary and Proof Requirements (1907–1917 Style; Evidence Heavy)

An alien shall be excluded as inadmissible unless the alien presents, at the time and in the manner required by this Act and implementing regulations, the documents required for the admission class claimed.

Required documents include, as applicable:

(1) Immediate Relative Class (a) Proof of qualifying relationship to a U.S. citizen (birth/marriage evidence and required civil-record equivalents as required by this Act or regulations);
(b) Proof of identity;
(c) Required immigration forms and sworn statements prescribed by this Act or regulations;
(d) Required medical examination results and specified health records where prescribed.

(2) Family-Preference Classes (a) Proof of qualifying family relationship (marriage, birth, and civil-record evidence as applicable);
(b) Proof of sponsor eligibility (including proof the sponsor is a U.S. citizen and meets statutory conditions applicable under this Act);
(c) Proof of identity;
(d) Proof of supporting relationship documentation, including prior marriages and divorces where applicable;
(e) Required medical examination results and specified health records where prescribed.

(3) Skilled Worker Classes (a) Proof of identity;
(b) Documentary evidence supporting the eligibility basis for the skilled worker class claimed;
(c) Documentary evidence of qualifications/credentials (licenses, diplomas, certificates, or other credentials required by this Act or regulations);
(d) Documentary evidence of any required employment offer or job-category eligibility where applicable;
(e) Required medical examination results and specified health records where prescribed;
(f) Any additional background/character-related documentation required by this Act for this class.

(4) Employer-Sponsored Occupational Classes (a) Proof that the employer meets eligibility requirements and registration/standing rules under this Act;
(b) Proof of job offer and occupational requirements;
(c) Proof the alien meets occupational qualifications (credentials);
(d) Proof of identity;
(e) Required medical examination results and specified health records where prescribed;
(f) Any additional supporting attestations and documentation required by this Act or regulations.

(5) Limited Unskilled or Contingent Labor Classes (a) Proof of identity;
(b) Proof the alien falls within only the limited statutory circumstances expressly authorized by this Act for such class;
(c) Proof of the documented labor basis required by this Act for the class;
(d) Required medical examination results and specified health records where prescribed;
(e) Required evidence that the proposed arrangement is lawful under this Act.

(6) Humanitarian Protection Classes (Refugee/Asylee Style; Quota-Limited) (a) Certified humanitarian processing documentation required by this Act and implementing regulations;
(b) Identity and background records required by this Act;
(c) Required medical examination results and specified health records;
(d) Security and character-related documentation required by this Act.

Documentary proof shall include translations and certified copies where required by this Act or regulations.

An alien shall be excluded if the alien’s documents are missing, inconsistent, materially incomplete, unverifiable, forged, counterfeit, or not in the form required by this Act or regulations.

Section 6. Numerical Limitation; Quotas (1921–1965 Style; Filled Categories)

Admission of aliens under this Act shall be limited by annual quotas established by the Secretary for each admission category and each admission class defined by this Act.

Admissions shall be authorized only through the following admission categories:

(1) Immediate Relative Category
Admission for qualifying immediate relatives as defined by this Act, subject to this Act’s documentary and inadmissibility requirements.

(2) Family-Preference Categories
Admission for qualifying relatives other than immediate relatives, limited to family-preference subcategories expressly authorized by this Act: (A) Spouse of a U.S. citizen;
(B) Unmarried adult child of a U.S. citizen;
(C) Married child of a U.S. citizen;
(D) Sibling of a U.S. citizen;
(E) Other family relationships only to the extent expressly enumerated in this Act.

(3) Skilled Worker Categories
Admission for workers meeting legislatively defined skills and credential requirements and providing documents required by this Act.

(4) Employer-Sponsored Occupational Categories
Admission for aliens where the employer meets eligibility requirements and the alien’s qualifications are documented under this Act.

(5) Limited Unskilled or Contingent Labor Categories
Admission only in limited circumstances expressly authorized by this Act under strict documentation and eligibility requirements.

(6) Humanitarian Protection Categories (Refugee/Asylee Style; Quota-Limited)
Admission only through designated quota-limited humanitarian classes administered under procedures provided by this Act.

(7) Other Enumerated Categories
No additional category shall be created except by Act of Congress and only for the purposes and limits expressly authorized.

Quota Suspension. If admissions in any category and admission class reach the quota ceiling established under this Act, no further admissions may occur under that category and admission class until the next quota year or until Congress authorizes an adjustment under this Act.

Unused quota numbers may be reallocated only if Congress expressly authorizes such reallocation, and only in a manner that does not expand admission beyond the purpose of this Act.

Section 7. Admission Classes (Quota/Eligibility Implementation; Filled Class Names)

This Act establishes the following admission classes: (1) Immediate Relative Class
For aliens meeting the Immediate Relative Category and presenting required documents and proving non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

(2) Family-Preference Classes
For aliens meeting one of the family-preference subcategories in Section 6(2) and presenting required documents and proving non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

(3) Skilled Worker Classes
For aliens meeting skilled worker requirements, presenting required credentials and any required employment/job-category eligibility documents, and proving non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

(4) Employer-Sponsored Occupational Classes
For aliens whose employers meet eligibility requirements, whose job offer and occupational requirements are documented, whose credentials are documented, and who prove non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

(5) Limited Unskilled or Contingent Labor Classes
For aliens meeting limited statutory circumstances expressly authorized for this Act’s unskilled/contingent-labor framework and presenting required proof and proving non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

(6) Humanitarian Protection Classes
For aliens processed under quota-limited humanitarian procedures and presenting required humanitarian documentation, identity and background records, and proving non-inadmissibility under Sections 3–5.

No Admission Without Express Class Authorization. An alien who does not qualify for an admission class expressly established in this Act shall be excluded.

Section 8. Medical + Eligibility Interaction (Admissions Must Clear Both Proof and Admissibility)

Admission under any quota/eligibility class is conditioned upon compliance with Sections 3–5.
If an alien is found inadmissible under Section 3 (medical) or Section 4 (character) or is excluded under Section 5 (documentary/proof requirements), the alien shall be excluded regardless of quota or category availability.
If an alien fails to establish eligibility for the claimed admission class under Section 6 and Section 7 (including quota/class rules), the alien shall be excluded regardless of any medical or documentary partial sufficiency.

Section 9. Carrier Obligations and Enforcement (Restrictive Compliance)

Carriers shall be required to verify, to the maximum extent permitted by law and regulation, that transported persons comply with documentary requirements of this Act as applicable.
Carriers transporting aliens who are excluded or inadmissible under this Act shall be subject to penalties and liabilities as prescribed by this Act and regulations.
The Secretary may require additional documentary verification mechanisms by carriers consistent with this Act.

Section 10. Fraud, Misrepresentation, and Enhanced Denial Bars

An alien who obtains (or attempts to obtain) admission by fraud or willful misrepresentation shall be excluded.
Material omissions shall be treated as misrepresentation where the omitted facts would have affected eligibility, quota authorization, or inadmissibility determinations.
Repeat or aggravated fraud shall result in enhanced restrictions on eligibility for admission under this Act consistent with the purpose of this Act.

Section 11. Deportation and Removal (Restrictive Enforcement Authority)

An alien shall be removable if the alien meets grounds for removal as provided by this Act and regulations issued pursuant to this Act.
The Secretary may authorize detention during proceedings consistent with procedures

posts.raw_content: "The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act is set to automatically become law at midnight on Friday, July 10, 2026, without President Donald Trump's signature.  Under the U.S. Constitution, legislation becomes law if the president neither signs nor vetoes it within 10 days (excluding Sundays) while Congress remains in session.  Although Trump canceled a scheduled signing ceremony on June 24 to pressure Congress on the unrelated SAVE America Act, he has not issued a veto, allowing the bill to pass via constitutional default.
posts.author: Bullet3250
posts.title: Trump needs to VETO THE HOUSING BILL.
posts.content: "The 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act is set to automatically become law at midnight on Friday, July 10, 2026, without President Donald Trump's signature. Under the U.S. Constitution, legislation becomes law if the president neither signs nor vetoes it within 10 days (excluding Sundays) while Congress remains in session. Although Trump canceled a scheduled signing ceremony on June 24 to pressure Congress on the unrelated SAVE America Act, he has not issued a veto, allowing the bill to pass via constitutional default.
